stem: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the regional emergency department by his mother with a 3-week history of polyuria, nocturia, polydipsia and a 3 kg unintentional weight loss. He has been more tired than usual but is still attending school and eating normally. He has no abdominal pain, no vomiting and no Kussmaul respiration. He has no past medical history, takes no medications, is fully immunised, and his maternal aunt has type 1 diabetes. On examination he is alert and well perfused with no signs of dehydration. A fingerprick blood glucose is 22 mmol/L and capillary blood ketones are 1.8 mmol/L. A venous blood gas shows pH 7.35 and bicarbonate 19 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 96 bpm, RR 18 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 108/66 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Alert, GCS 15, mucous membranes moist, capillary refill <2 seconds, no acetone breath, abdomen soft non-tender
data_table.Investigations: Capillary BSL 22 mmol/L, blood ketones 1.8 mmol/L, VBG pH 7.35, HCO3 19 mmol/L, Na 138 mmol/L, K 4.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge home with a urine dipstick, GP referral for fasting glucose and HbA1c in the morning
options.rationale: Symptomatic hyperglycaemia with ketonaemia mandates same-day specialist paediatric review for diagnosis confirmation, education and insulin initiation, regardless of pH per RCH CPG: Diabetes Mellitus.

options.text: Commence intravenous 0.9% saline at twice maintenance and a fixed-rate insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/h
options.rationale: DKA-protocol fluid and insulin infusion are reserved for pH <7.30 or HCO3 <15 mmol/L; this child does not meet biochemical DKA criteria per ISPAD 2022 (AU adapted).

options.text: Admit under the paediatric diabetes team for confirmation, subcutaneous insulin initiation and structured education
options.rationale: New-onset T1DM without DKA (pH >=7.30, HCO3 >=15, ketones present but child clinically well) is admitted for subcutaneous insulin initiation, education and family training as per RCH CPG: Diabetes Mellitus and APEG handbook.

options.text: Give a single oral dose of metformin 500 mg and arrange paediatric endocrinology outpatient follow-up
options.rationale: Metformin is not first-line in suspected paediatric T1DM; insulin is required and oral hypoglycaemics have no role in initial management per APEG handbook.

options.text: Observe in the short-stay unit for 4 hours, repeat the glucose and discharge if it falls below 15 mmol/L
options.rationale: Glucose will not self-correct without exogenous insulin in T1DM and short-stay observation delays definitive management and education per RCH CPG: Diabetes Mellitus.

explanation.summary: New-onset T1DM in a clinically well child without DKA (pH >=7.30, HCO3 >=15) is admitted for subcutaneous insulin commencement, glucose monitoring training and structured family education. DKA biochemical criteria are pH <7.30 or HCO3 <15 mmol/L with ketonaemia.
key_points: DKA criteria (ISPAD): hyperglycaemia >11 mmol/L + ketonaemia + pH <7.30 or HCO3 <15
Without DKA, use subcutaneous insulin and oral hydration
Outpatient initiation is not appropriate in Australian paediatric practice
Education covers carbohydrate counting, hypoglycaemia recognition, sick day rules
explanation.pearls: A pH of exactly 7.35 with bicarbonate 19 is not DKA. Do not start a DKA protocol on a child who does not need one.

stem: A 12-year-old girl weighing 40 kg with a 2-week history of polyuria and polydipsia presents to a tertiary paediatric emergency department after a day of vomiting and abdominal pain. She is drowsy but rousable, with deep sighing respirations and acetone breath. Capillary glucose is 32 mmol/L and blood ketones are 5.8 mmol/L. A venous blood gas shows pH 7.08, HCO3 7 mmol/L, Na 132 mmol/L, K 4.4 mmol/L, chloride 102 mmol/L, urea 9.8 mmol/L and creatinine 88 micromol/L. She has not previously been diagnosed with diabetes and is not on any chronic medication. There is no recent head injury, no seizure activity, and no concurrent gastrointestinal infection in the family. The senior registrar has commenced a 10 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% saline over 30 minutes for clinically estimated 8 percent dehydration, an indwelling urinary catheter has been placed for hourly urine output measurement, and a second wide-bore IV cannula has been sited. The on-call paediatric endocrinologist and the PICU team have been informed.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132 bpm, RR 36 /min Kussmaul, BP 96/58 mmHg, SpO2 99% on RA, T 37.0 deg C, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6)
data_table.Examination: Sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 3 seconds, abdomen soft mildly tender, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: BSL 32 mmol/L, ketones 5.8 mmol/L, VBG pH 7.08, HCO3 7, Na 132, K 4.4, Cl 102, urea 9.8, creatinine 88 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate insulin and fluid management plan immediately after the resuscitation bolus?

options.text: Commence an insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/h immediately with the resuscitation bolus and start maintenance fluids only after the second hour
options.rationale: Insulin should not start until at least 1 hour after fluid resuscitation has begun, because early insulin increases the risk of cerebral oedema and rapid osmolar shifts per RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis and ISPAD 2022.

options.text: Give an IV insulin bolus of 0.1 units/kg followed by an infusion at 0.1 units/kg/h and replace the deficit over 24 hours
options.rationale: IV insulin boluses are contraindicated in paediatric DKA because of the cerebral oedema risk, and rapid deficit replacement (<48 hours) increases osmolar shifts per ISPAD 2022.

options.text: Use 5% dextrose with 0.45% saline from the outset and an insulin infusion at 0.15 units/kg/h
options.rationale: Adding dextrose before glucose has fallen below 14-17 mmol/L is inappropriate, and 0.15 units/kg/h exceeds the recommended 0.05-0.1 units/kg/h ceiling per RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis.

options.text: Start 0.9% saline maintenance plus calculated deficit replaced evenly over 48 hours, with insulin infusion 0.05-0.1 units/kg/h commenced 1-2 hours after fluid started
options.rationale: ISPAD and the RCH DKA pathway use 0.9% saline (transitioned to 0.45% saline with 5% dextrose once glucose <14-17 mmol/L), with the calculated deficit replaced over 48 hours and insulin starting 1-2 hours after fluid resuscitation at 0.05-0.1 units/kg/h to reduce cerebral oedema risk.

options.text: Replace the deficit with 0.9% saline over 6 hours and start an insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/h once the bolus is complete
options.rationale: Rapid 6-hour deficit replacement and simultaneous insulin commencement substantially increase cerebral oedema risk; deficit is replaced over 48 hours per ISPAD 2022.

explanation.summary: Paediatric DKA: assume 5-8% dehydration; give one or two 10 mL/kg boluses of 0.9% saline only if shocked; replace remaining deficit over 48 hours; start insulin at 0.05-0.1 units/kg/h 1-2 hours after fluid resuscitation begins; switch fluid to 0.45% saline with 5% dextrose once glucose falls below 14-17 mmol/L; monitor neuro hourly.
key_points: Cerebral oedema risk factors: young age, new-onset DM, severe acidosis, low pCO2, high urea, bicarbonate therapy, rapid fluid, early insulin
Do not give insulin bolus
Replace K early once urine output established
Watch for headache, GCS drop, bradycardia, hypertension (Cushing response)
explanation.pearls: Cerebral oedema typically occurs 4-12 hours into therapy. The temptation to fix the numbers fast is the danger.

stem: A 9-year-old boy weighing 30 kg is 6 hours into treatment of newly diagnosed diabetic ketoacidosis in a paediatric high dependency unit at a tertiary children's hospital. His initial pH was 7.02 with HCO3 5 mmol/L, glucose 38 mmol/L, urea 12 mmol/L, creatinine 84 micromol/L, sodium 134 mmol/L (corrected sodium 142 mmol/L), potassium 4.6 mmol/L and chloride 100 mmol/L. He received a single 10 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% saline over 30 minutes, then maintenance fluid plus 48-hour deficit replacement using 0.9% saline with potassium chloride 40 mmol/L, and an insulin infusion at 0.05 units/kg/h started 90 minutes after fluids commenced. He has not received any sodium bicarbonate. Glucose is now 16 mmol/L, pH 7.18 and HCO3 11 mmol/L, and the nurses have just transitioned the fluid to 0.45% saline with 5% dextrose. Over the last 30 minutes the bedside nurse has noted progressively increasing irritability, a new complaint of headache, and now a single episode of non-bilious vomiting. On urgent reassessment his GCS has fallen from 15 to 11 (E3 V3 M5), with a heart rate that has slowed from 120 to 78 bpm and a rising blood pressure of 138/92 mmHg. Pupils are 4 mm and sluggish bilaterally with no focal motor deficit and no posturing. The bedside glucose is 14 mmol/L and the most recent potassium was 3.9 mmol/L. The consultant intensivist is en route from the ICU upstairs, the airway trolley with appropriately sized equipment is at the bedside, and 3% saline and mannitol are available in the resus bay.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 bpm (down from 120), BP 138/92 mmHg (up from 108/66), RR 14 /min, SpO2 98% on RA, T 36.9 deg C, GCS 11 (E3 V3 M5)
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, headache, one vomit, pupils 4 mm sluggish bilaterally, no focal neurology, fontanelle closed, no papilloedema visible
data_table.Investigations: BSL 14 mmol/L, K 3.9 mmol/L, last VBG pH 7.18 HCO3 11, urine output 1.5 mL/kg/h

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Increase IV fluid rate to twice maintenance and rebolus 10 mL/kg of 0.9% saline to address suspected hypovolaemia
options.rationale: Bradycardia with hypertension and falling GCS is the Cushing response indicating raised intracranial pressure from cerebral oedema; fluid expansion worsens cerebral oedema, and the fluid rate must be reduced not increased per RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis.

options.text: Stop the insulin infusion and give an IV bolus of 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg for hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Glucose is 14 mmol/L and is not the cause of declining GCS; stopping insulin worsens ketosis, and the clinical picture points to cerebral oedema not hypoglycaemia per ISPAD 2022.

options.text: Give 3% hypertonic saline 5 mL/kg IV over 15 minutes, reduce IV fluid rate by one third, head up 30 degrees, urgent CT brain after stabilisation
options.rationale: Suspected DKA-associated cerebral oedema (declining GCS, headache, Cushing response, vomiting) is treated immediately with hypertonic saline 2.7-3% at 2.5-5 mL/kg over 10-15 minutes (or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg), reduction of IV fluid rate by one third, head-up 30 degrees, and neuroimaging only after stabilisation per RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis and ISPAD 2022.

options.text: Administer IV sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg over 30 minutes to correct the residual acidosis
options.rationale: Bicarbonate administration is an independent risk factor for DKA-associated cerebral oedema and is not indicated; pH is already improving to 7.18 per ISPAD 2022.

options.text: Perform a lumbar puncture to exclude bacterial meningitis as the cause of altered consciousness
options.rationale: LP in raised intracranial pressure risks coning and would delay osmotherapy; the clinical picture (Cushing response in established DKA) is cerebral oedema per RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis.

explanation.summary: DKA-associated cerebral oedema occurs in approximately 0.5-1% of paediatric DKA episodes, typically 4-12 hours into therapy, and has a 20-25% mortality. Treatment is immediate hypertonic saline 3% (2.5-5 mL/kg over 10-15 min) or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg over 15-20 min, head up 30 degrees, reduction of fluid rate by one third, and urgent intensivist input. CT is only after stabilisation.
key_points: Clinical diagnostic criteria (Muir): abnormal motor/verbal response, decorticate or decerebrate posturing, cranial nerve palsy, abnormal respiratory pattern
Hypertonic saline 3% 2.5-5 mL/kg OR mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg
Reduce maintenance fluid rate by one third
Avoid hyperventilation below pCO2 35 mmHg unless herniating
explanation.pearls: A bradycardia in a child who was just tachycardic in DKA is cerebral oedema until proven otherwise. Treat first, image after.

stem: A 6-year-old girl with T1DM diagnosed 8 months ago is found by her father at home unresponsive in bed at 06:30. She is on a basal-bolus regimen of insulin glargine 8 units at dinner and insulin aspart with meals, and she had a heavier than usual swimming session yesterday afternoon. Her father estimates her current weight at 22 kg. He checks her capillary glucose, which reads 1.6 mmol/L. She is breathing normally and has a strong pulse, but she does not respond to her name, withdraws only to a sternal rub, and is having intermittent generalised tonic-clonic jerking. There is no IV access available at home. He has an in-date glucagon hypokit and an oral glucose gel in the fridge. He has phoned the ambulance, which is 12 minutes away.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138 bpm, RR 22 /min, SpO2 97% on RA, T 36.4 deg C
data_table.Examination: Unresponsive to voice, withdraws to pain, intermittent generalised tonic-clonic activity, airway patent

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate action by her father?

options.text: Place oral glucose gel between her cheek and gum and roll her onto her side
options.rationale: Oral glucose is contraindicated in a child with reduced conscious level or active seizure because of aspiration risk; parenteral glucagon is indicated per RCH CPG: Hypoglycaemia and APEG handbook.

options.text: Administer intramuscular glucagon 1 mg and give a further injection of insulin aspart 2 units to stabilise the response
options.rationale: 1 mg glucagon is the dose for children 25 kg or more; insulin is contraindicated in active hypoglycaemia per RCH CPG: Hypoglycaemia.

options.text: Wait for the ambulance to arrive and administer the glucagon under paramedic supervision in 12 minutes
options.rationale: Severe hypoglycaemia with seizure requires immediate treatment; delay risks hypoxic brain injury and prolonged seizure per APEG handbook.

options.text: Administer intramuscular glucagon 0.5 mg from the hypokit and place her in the recovery position
options.rationale: Severe hypoglycaemia (unconscious or seizing) in a child under 25 kg is treated with IM glucagon 0.5 mg; the dose is 1 mg for children >=25 kg, with recovery position and ambulance attendance per RCH CPG: Hypoglycaemia.

options.text: Give a buccal dose of midazolam 0.3 mg/kg to terminate the seizure before considering glucose correction
options.rationale: Hypoglycaemic seizure terminates with glucose, not benzodiazepine; midazolam without correcting glucose risks airway compromise per RCH CPG: Hypoglycaemia.

explanation.summary: Severe hypoglycaemia (BSL <4 mmol/L with reduced consciousness or seizure) in a child with T1DM is treated outside hospital with IM glucagon: 0.5 mg if <25 kg or <8 years, 1 mg if >=25 kg. In hospital, 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg IV is preferred. Mild hypoglycaemia (conscious, able to swallow) is treated with 10-15 g rapid-acting oral carbohydrate, then complex carbohydrate.
key_points: Treatment threshold BSL <4 mmol/L
Glucagon 0.5 mg <25 kg, 1 mg >=25 kg
Repeat BSL 15 minutes after treatment
Investigate cause: missed meal, exercise, insulin dose error, illness
explanation.pearls: Glucagon does not work well in glycogen-depleted states (alcohol, prolonged starvation, repeated hypoglycaemia). Give IV dextrose if available.

stem: A 12-day-old male term infant is brought to the regional emergency department with three days of poor feeding, vomiting and increasing lethargy. He was born at 39+2 by NVD with a birth weight of 3.4 kg, was exclusively breastfed, and was discharged at 48 hours with a normal newborn examination and a heel-prick screen that has not yet returned. He has now lost 14% of his birth weight. There is no diarrhoea, no fevers, and no sick contacts. On examination he is pale, peripherally cool with capillary refill of 4 seconds, and has sunken fontanelle and dry mucous membranes. The genitalia appear normal male with bilaterally descended testes and no hyperpigmentation noted by the parents. Bloods drawn on arrival show Na 124 mmol/L, K 7.2 mmol/L, glucose 2.4 mmol/L, urea 11 mmol/L, creatinine 62 micromol/L, and a venous blood gas with pH 7.22, HCO3 14 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 188 bpm, RR 56 /min, SpO2 96% on RA, T 36.2 deg C, BP 62/38 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Lethargic, sunken anterior fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 4 seconds, normal male external genitalia
data_table.Investigations: Na 124 mmol/L, K 7.2 mmol/L, glucose 2.4 mmol/L, urea 11 mmol/L, creatinine 62 micromol/L, pH 7.22, HCO3 14 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most likely underlying diagnosis?

options.text: Pyloric stenosis with hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis
options.rationale: Pyloric stenosis produces a hypochloraemic hypokalaemic alkalosis with non-bilious vomiting at 3-6 weeks; this infant has hyperkalaemia, hyponatraemia and a metabolic acidosis per BMJ Best Practice: Pyloric stenosis.

options.text: Late-onset group B streptococcal sepsis with septic shock and acute kidney injury
options.rationale: GBS sepsis can present with shock at this age but does not typically produce isolated hyperkalaemia with hyponatraemia in this pattern; the electrolyte signature is the discriminator per RCH CPG: Neonatal Sepsis.

options.text: Inborn error of metabolism with urea cycle defect and hyperammonaemic encephalopathy
options.rationale: Urea cycle defects produce respiratory alkalosis with hyperammonaemia and not hyperkalaemia with metabolic acidosis as the primary picture per StatPearls: Urea Cycle Disorders.

options.text: Pseudohypoaldosteronism type 1 due to renal mineralocorticoid resistance
options.rationale: PHA1 is a rare differential with the same biochemistry but is much less common than 21-hydroxylase deficiency and is excluded by elevated 17-OH progesterone and cortisol response per ESPE 2018.

options.text: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia due to 21-hydroxylase deficiency with a salt-wasting crisis
options.rationale: Day 7-14 presentation with hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia, metabolic acidosis and shock in either sex is classical for salt-wasting CAH from 21-hydroxylase deficiency; males have normal external genitalia and so are missed clinically until biochemical crisis per RCH CPG: Adrenal crisis and ESPE 2018 CAH consensus.

explanation.summary: Classical salt-wasting 21-hydroxylase deficiency presents at day 7-14 with vomiting, lethargy, weight loss, dehydration and the characteristic electrolyte triad of hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia and hypoglycaemia with metabolic acidosis. Females are usually identified earlier from virilised genitalia; males present in crisis. Diagnosis is by markedly elevated 17-OH progesterone. Treatment is IV fluids, hydrocortisone, dextrose, and long-term hydrocortisone plus fludrocortisone.
key_points: Newborn screen (when returned) shows raised 17-OH progesterone
Salt-wasting crisis day 7-14 is the classic timing
Initial bolus 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline, then hydrocortisone 50-100 mg/m^2 IV
Long-term: hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone + salt supplementation in infancy
explanation.pearls: Any unwell second-week-of-life infant with hyponatraemia and hyperkalaemia is CAH until proven otherwise. The genitalia look normal in boys.

stem: A 4-year-old girl with classical salt-wasting congenital adrenal hyperplasia is brought to the paediatric emergency department by her parents with 24 hours of fever, vomiting and reduced oral intake during a gastroenteritis outbreak at her childcare centre. Her regular medications are hydrocortisone 5 mg in the morning, 2.5 mg at midday and 5 mg at night, plus fludrocortisone 100 microg daily. Her parents administered an extra oral hydrocortisone dose 6 hours ago but she vomited it back within 10 minutes and has not tolerated anything orally since. They did not have an in-date intramuscular hydrocortisone ampoule at home. On arrival she is drowsy and pale with markedly delayed capillary refill of 4 seconds, cool peripheries and a thready pulse. Her body surface area is calculated at 0.7 m^2 and her weight today is 16 kg. Bedside glucose is 2.8 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, RR 38 /min, SpO2 98% on RA, T 38.7 deg C, BP 78/44 mmHg, GCS 13
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, mottled peripheries, capillary refill 4 seconds, dry mucous membranes, abdomen soft non-tender
data_table.Investigations: Capillary glucose 2.8 mmol/L, point-of-care Na 130 mmol/L, K 5.4 mmol/L, weight 16 kg, BSA 0.7 m^2

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Oral hydrocortisone 20 mg with oral rehydration solution and observe in short-stay for 4 hours
options.rationale: She is shocked, hypoglycaemic and vomiting; oral therapy is inappropriate and parenteral hydrocortisone with IV resuscitation is required per RCH CPG: Adrenal Crisis.

options.text: IV dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg, 10 mL/kg Hartmann's solution and IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg
options.rationale: Dexamethasone has no mineralocorticoid activity and is unsuitable for adrenal crisis; hydrocortisone provides both glucocorticoid and at high dose mineralocorticoid effect per RCH CPG: Adrenal Crisis.

options.text: IV hydrocortisone 50 mg bolus, 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline IV bolus, and 2 mL/kg of 10% dextrose for hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis management is stress-dose IV hydrocortisone 50-100 mg/m^2 bolus (approximately 50 mg for a BSA of 0.7 m^2, or 2 mg/kg if BSA unknown), 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus, and 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg for documented hypoglycaemia per RCH CPG: Adrenal Crisis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

options.text: IM hydrocortisone 100 mg and oral fludrocortisone 100 microg as a double daily dose
options.rationale: IM hydrocortisone is acceptable if no IV access, but IV is preferred when access is available; oral fludrocortisone does not address the acute crisis and is not stress-dosed per APEG position statement on adrenal insufficiency.

options.text: IV methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg and a 20 mL/kg 3% saline bolus for presumed cerebral oedema
options.rationale: Methylprednisolone is not the preferred replacement steroid; hypertonic saline is not indicated as she does not have cerebral oedema, only mild hyponatraemia per RCH CPG: Adrenal Crisis.

explanation.summary: Stress-dose hydrocortisone for paediatric adrenal crisis is 50-100 mg/m^2 IV (or 2 mg/kg if BSA unknown), followed by 50-100 mg/m^2/day in divided doses. Resuscitation is 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline, with 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg for hypoglycaemia. Fludrocortisone is not needed acutely because high-dose hydrocortisone has sufficient mineralocorticoid effect.
key_points: Bolus: 50 mg/m^2 IV or 2 mg/kg
Maintenance: 50-100 mg/m^2/day divided 4-6 hourly
Fluids: 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline; 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg if BSL <3.0
Dexamethasone is for diagnostic testing only, not crisis management
explanation.pearls: Every CAH family should carry an in-date IM hydrocortisone ampoule and have been trained in its use. Sick day rules: double or triple oral dose, switch to IM if vomiting.

stem: An 11-year-old girl is referred by her GP for short stature. Her height has been below the 1st centile since age 5, and over the last 12 months her growth velocity has been 3 cm/year, having previously tracked along the 3rd centile. Her mid-parental height projects to the 25th centile. Her mother had menarche at age 13 and her father had a normal pubertal timing. She has no chronic medications, no fatigue, no constipation, no abdominal symptoms, and no family history of coeliac disease, IBD or thyroid disease. She has not yet had any breast development and there is no axillary or pubic hair. Her weight is on the 10th centile and BMI is normal. Examination shows a normal cardiovascular system with no radio-femoral delay or murmur, no goitre, no neck webbing, no cubitus valgus and a normal neurological examination. Bone age performed last week is 9 years.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 80 bpm, BP 104/64 mmHg, T 36.6 deg C, SpO2 99%
data_table.Examination: Height below 1st centile, weight 10th centile, Tanner stage 1 breast and pubic hair, no dysmorphic features, no goitre, no cubitus valgus
data_table.Investigations: Bone age 9 years (chronological 11), MPH 25th centile, growth velocity 3 cm/year

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate first-line investigation panel?

options.text: Growth hormone provocation testing with clonidine and arginine stimulation alone
options.rationale: GH provocation testing is second-line after exclusion of systemic disease, hypothyroidism, coeliac disease and Turner syndrome; first-line is a broad screening panel per RCH CPG: Short Stature and APEG growth guidance.

options.text: Full blood count, urea/electrolytes/creatinine, LFT, TSH/free T4, coeliac serology (tTG-IgA + total IgA), IGF-1, and karyotype
options.rationale: The standard first-line short-stature workup includes FBC, UEC, LFT, TSH/T4, coeliac serology, IGF-1, bone age and karyotype (in girls) to exclude systemic illness, hypothyroidism, coeliac, GH deficiency and Turner syndrome before specialist provocation testing per RCH CPG: Short Stature and ESPE consensus.

options.text: MRI brain with pituitary protocol and dynamic GnRH stimulation testing
options.rationale: Pituitary MRI is indicated after biochemical confirmation of GH deficiency or if neurological features are present, not as a first-line screen per RCH CPG: Short Stature.

options.text: Random cortisol, ACTH and a short Synacthen test to exclude Addison's disease as a cause of short stature
options.rationale: Addison's disease is not a typical cause of isolated short stature with normal weight; targeted adrenal testing is not first-line per APEG growth guidance.

options.text: Faecal calprotectin and an upper GI endoscopy to exclude inflammatory bowel disease as the cause
options.rationale: IBD workup is targeted to GI symptoms or abnormal first-line bloods; endoscopy is invasive and not a screening test per BMJ Best Practice: Short Stature.

explanation.summary: First-line workup of short stature in a girl: FBC, UEC, LFT, TSH/free T4, coeliac serology (tTG-IgA + total IgA), IGF-1 with IGFBP-3, vitamin D, bone age, and karyotype for any girl with unexplained short stature (Turner can present without classical features). Specialist GH testing and pituitary MRI are reserved for after this panel narrows the differential.
key_points: Growth velocity <5 cm/year between ages 4 and puberty is abnormal
Bone age delay >2 years suggests constitutional delay, hypothyroidism, GH deficiency or chronic illness
Karyotype mandatory in all unexplained short girls to exclude Turner
Mid-parental height = (mother + father)/2, minus 6.5 cm for girls
explanation.pearls: Turner syndrome can have a completely normal phenotype apart from short stature. Karyotype any girl who deserves a workup.

stem: A 6-year-old girl is referred by her GP for breast development first noticed by her mother 8 months ago. Her mother also reports the recent appearance of fine axillary hair, mild adult-type body odour, and accelerated linear growth (she has crossed two centile lines on the height chart in the past year, from 50th to 90th centile). She has had no headaches, no visual symptoms, no seizures, no abdominal pain and no vaginal bleeding. There is no neonatal history of seizures or hypothalamic insult. There is no exposure to topical oestrogen, no parental medications she could be accessing, no oestrogen-containing supplements in the household, and no soy-heavy diet. Her past medical and developmental history is unremarkable and there is no family history of central or peripheral precocious puberty, McCune-Albright syndrome, neurofibromatosis or congenital adrenal hyperplasia. On examination she has Tanner stage 3 breast development with palpable glandular tissue, Tanner stage 2 pubic hair, fine axillary hair on inspection of the underarm, no acne, no cafe-au-lait macules, no neurofibromas and no clitoromegaly. The thyroid is not palpable, the abdomen is soft with no palpable masses and no organomegaly, and the neurological examination including fundoscopy and visual fields by confrontation is normal. Bone age performed today is 9 years. Pelvic ultrasound shows a uterine length of 4.2 cm with an endometrial echo and bilateral ovaries with multiple small follicles and no ovarian cyst or mass.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 84 bpm, BP 102/62 mmHg, T 36.7 deg C, height 90th centile, weight 75th centile
data_table.Examination: Tanner B3 P2, fine axillary hair, mild body odour, no acne, no clitoromegaly, no abdominal mass, no cafe-au-lait macules
data_table.Investigations: Bone age 9 (chronological 6), pelvic US: uterus 4.2 cm with endometrial echo, ovaries bilateral follicles

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation to distinguish central from peripheral precocious puberty?

options.text: Pelvic MRI to look for an ovarian sex cord-stromal tumour producing oestradiol
options.rationale: The pelvic ultrasound has already shown a pubertal pattern without an ovarian mass; pelvic MRI is reserved for ultrasound abnormality or suspected peripheral cause per RCH CPG: Precocious Puberty.

options.text: GnRH (or GnRH analogue) stimulation test measuring LH and FSH at 0, 30 and 60 minutes
options.rationale: GnRH (or GnRH analogue) stimulation testing with peak LH >=5 IU/L (or LH:FSH ratio >0.66) confirms central precocious puberty, while a suppressed LH response with elevated oestradiol points to a peripheral cause per RCH CPG: Precocious Puberty and ESPE consensus.

options.text: Serum 17-OH progesterone and DHEAS to exclude non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia
options.rationale: Non-classical CAH causes premature adrenarche (pubic and axillary hair, body odour) without breast development; this child has Tanner B3 breast development, indicating gonadarche per ESPE consensus.

options.text: Empirical commencement of a GnRH analogue (leuprolide) and monitor for breast regression
options.rationale: Treatment should not precede confirmation of the diagnosis and underlying cause (including MRI brain for central PP in a girl under 6-8 years) per APEG position statement.

options.text: MRI brain with pituitary protocol to look for a hypothalamic hamartoma before any biochemical testing
options.rationale: MRI brain is performed once central precocious puberty is biochemically confirmed (or in girls under 6 with central features) but is not the first discriminating test between central and peripheral per RCH CPG: Precocious Puberty.

explanation.summary: Precocious puberty in girls is breast development before age 8 (or pubic hair/menarche before age 8/9). Central PP is gonadotropin-dependent (HPG axis activation); peripheral PP is gonadotropin-independent (ovarian, adrenal, exogenous). GnRH stimulation distinguishes the two: peak LH >=5 IU/L indicates central. Central PP in girls <6 years mandates MRI brain.
key_points: Random LH alone has poor sensitivity; stimulation is the test
Pubertal pelvic ultrasound: uterus >3.5-4 cm, endometrial echo, multifollicular ovaries
Bone age advancement >2 years suggests significant oestrogen exposure
Treatment for confirmed central PP is GnRH analogue to preserve adult height
explanation.pearls: Isolated premature thelarche (no growth spurt, no bone age advance, prepubertal LH response) does not need treatment, just surveillance.

stem: A 9-day-old female infant has a positive newborn screening result from the Victorian newborn bloodspot screening programme, with a TSH of 92 mIU/L on the day-2 sample (the laboratory cut-off used in Victoria is >=15 mIU/L on the dried bloodspot). She was born at 39+5 by uncomplicated NVD with a birth weight of 3.2 kg and Apgar scores of 9 and 9, has been exclusively breastfed every 3 hours, is feeding well, has had appropriate weight gain since the initial physiological loss and has passed meconium normally. She has mild prolonged jaundice with a transcutaneous bilirubin of 180 micromol/L at day 9 but is otherwise well, with no lethargy, no constipation, no hypothermia and no respiratory distress reported by her parents. On examination she is alert, has an open posterior fontanelle of 1 cm, no umbilical hernia, no macroglossia and no palpable goitre or anterior neck mass. Confirmatory venous bloods drawn today show TSH 88 mIU/L, free T4 5 pmol/L and thyroglobulin 12 microg/L, with maternal thyroid antibodies still pending. A thyroid ultrasound and Tc-99m pertechnetate scan are being arranged through the nuclear medicine department.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132 bpm, RR 38 /min, SpO2 100% on RA, T 36.8 deg C, weight 3.25 kg
data_table.Examination: Alert, mildly jaundiced, posterior fontanelle 1 cm, no macroglossia, no umbilical hernia, no goitre
data_table.Investigations: Bloodspot TSH 92 mIU/L, venous TSH 88 mIU/L, free T4 5 pmol/L, thyroglobulin 12 microg/L, bilirubin 180 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat the bloodspot TSH at 4 weeks of age and only treat if it remains elevated
options.rationale: A confirmed venous TSH of 88 with free T4 of 5 is unequivocal congenital hypothyroidism; delaying treatment by 3 weeks significantly worsens neurodevelopmental outcome per RCH CPG: Congenital Hypothyroidism and ESPE consensus.

options.text: Commence oral levothyroxine 10-15 microg/kg/day within 2 weeks of birth and review TFTs in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Confirmed congenital hypothyroidism requires levothyroxine 10-15 microg/kg/day commenced as soon as possible and ideally within 2 weeks of birth to optimise neurodevelopmental outcome, with TFT review at 2 weeks aiming for free T4 in the upper half of the reference range per RCH CPG: Congenital Hypothyroidism and ESPE-ATA consensus.

options.text: Commence oral levothyroxine 2 microg/kg/day to avoid overtreatment and review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: 2 microg/kg/day is the adult-equivalent maintenance dose and is markedly inadequate for neonatal congenital hypothyroidism, where 10-15 microg/kg/day is required per ESPE-ATA consensus.

options.text: Defer treatment pending the result of the thyroid scan to determine the underlying aetiology
options.rationale: Treatment should not be delayed for imaging; the scan can be performed concurrently or after stabilisation per RCH CPG: Congenital Hypothyroidism.

options.text: Commence intravenous liothyronine (T3) 2 microg/kg/day in preference to oral levothyroxine for faster onset
options.rationale: Oral levothyroxine is the standard treatment; T3 is reserved for myxoedema coma and is not used in neonatal CH per ESPE-ATA consensus.

explanation.summary: Congenital hypothyroidism affects approximately 1 in 2000 newborns in Australia. Treatment with levothyroxine 10-15 microg/kg/day started within 2 weeks of birth (ideally day 7-10) normalises IQ outcomes. Crushed tablets given with water or breast milk are preferred to liquid suspensions. Target free T4 in the upper half of reference range; aim TSH <5 mIU/L within 4 weeks.
key_points: Dose: levothyroxine 10-15 microg/kg/day oral
Aetiology: thyroid dysgenesis (~80%) > dyshormonogenesis > transient
Imaging: Tc-99m or I-123 scan + ultrasound to characterise
Re-evaluation off treatment at age 3 if dyshormonogenesis suspected
explanation.pearls: Avoid giving levothyroxine with soy-based formula, iron or calcium, which markedly reduce absorption.

stem: A 14-year-old girl is brought by her mother to the GP because of a 6-month history of fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, gradual weight gain of 4 kg with no change in appetite, and a recent slowing of menstruation since menarche at age 12. She has had no neck pain or dysphagia. There is a family history of autoimmune disease, with her aunt having coeliac disease and her grandmother having vitiligo. On examination she has a smooth, mildly enlarged, non-tender goitre approximately twice the normal size, dry skin, a delayed relaxation phase of the ankle reflex, and a heart rate of 56 bpm. Her height has tracked along the 25th centile but her growth velocity has fallen to 3 cm in the last year. Bloods today show TSH 78 mIU/L, free T4 6 pmol/L, anti-TPO antibodies 480 IU/mL (reference <35), anti-thyroglobulin antibodies 240 IU/mL, normal full blood count and normal electrolytes. Coeliac serology is pending.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 56 bpm, BP 108/68 mmHg, T 36.1 deg C, BMI 70th centile
data_table.Examination: Smooth diffuse non-tender goitre, dry skin, delayed ankle reflex relaxation, no exophthalmos, Tanner B4 P4
data_table.Investigations: TSH 78 mIU/L, free T4 6 pmol/L, anti-TPO 480 IU/mL, anti-Tg 240 IU/mL, FBC and UEC normal

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Commence oral levothyroxine 100 microg daily and recheck TFTs in 6-8 weeks
options.rationale: Starting at full adult replacement risks angina-like symptoms, tachyarrhythmia and behavioural symptoms in adolescents; titration from a lower dose is preferred unless severe symptoms per RCH CPG: Hypothyroidism and ESPE guidance.

options.text: Commence carbimazole 15 mg daily and propranolol 20 mg three times daily
options.rationale: Carbimazole and propranolol treat hyperthyroidism; her biochemistry shows hypothyroidism (high TSH, low free T4) per Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

options.text: Arrange thyroid ultrasound and fine-needle aspiration of the goitre before any treatment
options.rationale: A smooth diffuse goitre with positive anti-TPO antibodies is diagnostic of Hashimoto's thyroiditis; FNA is reserved for discrete nodules or rapidly growing goitre per RCH CPG: Hypothyroidism.

options.text: Defer treatment for 3 months to allow for possible spontaneous resolution of subclinical disease
options.rationale: This is overt (not subclinical) hypothyroidism with a TSH of 78 and a free T4 below the reference range, requiring treatment per ESPE consensus.

options.text: Commence oral levothyroxine 50 microg daily (approximately 1.2-1.7 microg/kg) and recheck TFTs in 6-8 weeks aiming for TSH in the lower half of reference range
options.rationale: Adolescent autoimmune (Hashimoto's) hypothyroidism is treated with levothyroxine 1.2-1.7 microg/kg/day (approximately 50 microg daily for a typical 14-year-old), titrating to TSH in the lower half of the reference range with TFTs at 6-8 week intervals per RCH CPG: Hypothyroidism and ESPE guidance.

explanation.summary: Hashimoto's thyroiditis is the commonest cause of acquired hypothyroidism in Australian adolescents, with a female predominance and associations with type 1 diabetes, coeliac disease and Turner syndrome. Treatment is levothyroxine titrated to TSH in the lower half of reference range, with biochemical monitoring at 6-8 weeks then every 6-12 months when stable.
key_points: Dose: 1.2-1.7 microg/kg/day in adolescents
Anti-TPO positive in approximately 90% of Hashimoto's
Screen for associated autoimmune conditions (T1DM, coeliac)
Recheck after illness, weight change, pregnancy or new interacting medication
explanation.pearls: TSH may take 4-6 weeks to normalise even with adequate dosing; don't escalate too quickly on a 2-week recheck.

stem: A 15-year-old girl is referred to the paediatric endocrinology outpatient clinic by her GP with a 3-month history of progressive unintentional weight loss of 6 kg despite increased appetite, heat intolerance, fine tremor, palpitations, mild anxiety, oligomenorrhoea and poor sleep, and a recent decline in academic performance noted by her teachers. Her mother has noticed that her eyes appear more prominent and that she has a fine tremor of the outstretched hands and a tendency to fidget. There is no recent viral illness, no anterior neck pain, no exogenous thyroid hormone or iodine-containing medication exposure, and no family history of thyroid disease, although a maternal cousin has type 1 diabetes mellitus and her grandmother has vitiligo. On examination she has a smooth diffuse non-tender goitre approximately twice normal size with an audible bruit on auscultation, mild bilateral proptosis with lid lag and lid retraction, fine resting tremor, warm sweaty palms, and a resting heart rate of 124 bpm in sinus rhythm. There is no pretibial myxoedema and no thyroid acropachy. Her bloods show TSH <0.01 mIU/L, free T4 48 pmol/L (reference 9-19), free T3 18 pmol/L (reference 3.5-6.5), anti-TSH receptor antibody 12 IU/L (reference <1.75), and a normal full blood count including a neutrophil count of 4.2 x 10^9/L with normal LFTs.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 124 bpm sinus, BP 138/72 mmHg with wide pulse pressure, T 37.4 deg C, RR 18 /min
data_table.Examination: Smooth goitre with bruit, mild bilateral proptosis with lid lag, fine tremor, warm sweaty palms, no pretibial myxoedema
data_table.Investigations: TSH <0.01 mIU/L, free T4 48 pmol/L, free T3 18 pmol/L, anti-TSHR Ab 12 IU/L, neutrophils 4.2 x 10^9/L, LFT normal

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate first-line medical management?

options.text: Propylthiouracil 5-10 mg/kg/day orally as the preferred thionamide because of its block of peripheral T4 to T3 conversion
options.rationale: PTU is contraindicated as first-line in paediatric Graves' due to a black-box warning for severe hepatotoxicity and acute liver failure; carbimazole is first-line per ESPE consensus.

options.text: Definitive radioactive iodine (I-131) ablation as initial therapy without a trial of thionamide
options.rationale: Initial therapy is medical with thionamide for 18-24 months; radioiodine is offered after relapse or in older adolescents with persistent disease per RCH CPG: Hyperthyroidism.

options.text: Total thyroidectomy under general anaesthesia as first-line definitive therapy
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for thionamide failure or intolerance, very large goitre, or Graves' ophthalmopathy where radioiodine is unsuitable per ESPE consensus.

options.text: Carbimazole 0.5-1 mg/kg/day orally in divided doses plus propranolol 1-2 mg/kg/day for symptomatic control, with neutrophil and LFT monitoring
options.rationale: Australian and ESPE-recommended first-line therapy for paediatric Graves' disease is a thionamide (carbimazole preferred to propylthiouracil because of PTU hepatotoxicity in children) at 0.5-1 mg/kg/day with a beta-blocker for adrenergic symptoms, baseline and serial neutrophil and LFT monitoring per RCH CPG: Hyperthyroidism and Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

options.text: Lugol's iodine 5-10 drops three times daily as monotherapy until normalisation of free T4
options.rationale: Inorganic iodine is used pre-operatively or in thyroid storm but not as monotherapy for chronic Graves' management per Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

explanation.summary: Paediatric Graves' disease accounts for approximately 95% of paediatric hyperthyroidism, with a strong female predominance. Initial therapy is carbimazole (preferred over PTU due to hepatotoxicity) for at least 18-24 months, with propranolol for adrenergic symptoms. Remission rate after a course is approximately 25-30%; relapse warrants discussion of definitive therapy (radioiodine or thyroidectomy).
key_points: Carbimazole 0.5-1 mg/kg/day, max 30 mg/day (titration regimen) or block-and-replace
PTU avoided in children except in thyroid storm or first trimester pregnancy
Monitor for agranulocytosis (FBC if sore throat or fever) and hepatotoxicity
Anti-TSHR Ab titre at end of treatment predicts relapse risk
explanation.pearls: Counsel families about agranulocytosis: any sore throat or fever on carbimazole = stop and check FBC.

stem: A 5-year-old boy is referred from his GP for evaluation of polyuria and polydipsia of 4 months' duration. His parents estimate his fluid intake at approximately 4 litres per day, including overnight water bottles, and he is waking 3-4 times nightly to drink and urinate, with secondary nocturnal enuresis after being dry at night for the previous 18 months. He has had a 1 kg weight loss but is otherwise growing on the 25th centile and his pubertal stage is Tanner 1 as expected. There is no history of head injury, no headaches, no visual disturbance, no anosmia and no symptoms of diabetes mellitus such as polyphagia. His past history includes Langerhans cell histiocytosis diagnosed 18 months ago at age 3 with cutaneous and bony involvement, treated with vinblastine and prednisolone, that has been in apparent remission on surveillance imaging. On examination he is alert, well perfused, with normal vital signs and an unremarkable systemic examination, including normal fundoscopy and visual field examination by confrontation. Bloods today show Na 148 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, urea 6.2 mmol/L, creatinine 38 micromol/L, glucose 4.8 mmol/L, calcium 2.4 mmol/L, plasma osmolality 305 mOsm/kg with a paired urine osmolality of 110 mOsm/kg measured on a morning sample. His urinary specific gravity is 1.002 and the urine dipstick is otherwise negative.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96 bpm, BP 104/64 mmHg, T 36.7 deg C, weight 18 kg
data_table.Examination: Alert, mucous membranes moist, no visual field defect, no papilloedema, no skin lesions visible
data_table.Investigations: Na 148 mmol/L, plasma osm 305 mOsm/kg, urine osm 110 mOsm/kg, SG 1.002, glucose 4.8 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Formal water deprivation test with paired plasma and urine osmolality, then desmopressin challenge
options.rationale: A water deprivation test risks dangerous dehydration when there is already biochemical evidence of inappropriate dilute urine in the face of hypernatraemia and hyperosmolar plasma; the diagnosis is already biochemically established and a desmopressin trial can proceed directly per RCH CPG: Diabetes Insipidus and ESPE consensus.
source_refs: ESPE Consensus on Central Diabetes Insipidus

options.text: Desmopressin trial with measurement of urine osmolality response, followed by MRI brain with pituitary protocol
options.rationale: When plasma is already hyperosmolar (>295 mOsm/kg) with hypernatraemia and inappropriately dilute urine (<300 mOsm/kg), DI is biochemically confirmed and a desmopressin trial (urine concentrates >50% = central DI; no response = nephrogenic DI) with MRI brain (looking for loss of posterior pituitary bright spot, infiltrative disease) is appropriate, particularly with this child's LCH history per RCH CPG: Diabetes Insipidus.
source_refs: ESPE Consensus on Central Diabetes Insipidus

options.text: Renal ultrasound and urinary tract DMSA scan to assess for medullary cystic disease
options.rationale: Renal imaging is reasonable later if nephrogenic DI is confirmed by no response to desmopressin, but is not the first-line discriminator per BMJ Best Practice: Diabetes Insipidus.

options.text: Oral glucose tolerance test to exclude type 1 diabetes mellitus as the cause
options.rationale: Random glucose of 4.8 mmol/L and a urine specific gravity of 1.002 exclude osmotic diuresis from glucose; this is hypotonic polyuria not diabetes mellitus per RCH CPG.

options.text: 24-hour urinary calcium and serum parathyroid hormone to exclude hypercalciuric nephrogenic DI
options.rationale: Calcium is normal at 2.4 mmol/L and chronic hypercalcaemia is an unlikely primary cause here; LCH-related central DI is the most likely diagnosis given the history per ESPE consensus.
source_refs: ESPE Consensus on Central Diabetes Insipidus

explanation.summary: Diabetes insipidus is suggested by hypotonic polyuria with hypernatraemia and hyperosmolar plasma (>295) with inappropriately dilute urine (<300). Once biochemically confirmed without need for water deprivation, a desmopressin trial distinguishes central (urine concentrates) from nephrogenic (no response). MRI brain is mandatory in confirmed central DI to exclude tumour, infiltration (LCH, germ cell tumour) or malformation.
key_points: Hypernatraemia should be corrected slowly (<10-12 mmol/L per 24 hours) to avoid cerebral oedema
Causes of central DI: idiopathic, tumour (craniopharyngioma, germinoma), LCH, trauma, infection
Nephrogenic DI: X-linked AVPR2 mutation, hypercalcaemia, hypokalaemia, lithium, sickle cell
Treatment of central DI: oral or intranasal desmopressin titrated to symptoms
explanation.pearls: LCH commonly involves the pituitary stalk and presents as central DI before any imaging-visible lesion. Repeat MRI 6-monthly if initial scan is normal.

sources.label: ESPE Consensus on Central Diabetes Insipidus

stem: A 7-year-old girl is on day 3 of admission for culture-proven Streptococcus pneumoniae meningitis. She is receiving IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg daily, has had dexamethasone for the first 4 doses, and is on maintenance IV fluids with 0.9% saline plus 5% dextrose at 70 mL/hour. Her GCS has improved from 12 on admission to 14 today. Overnight the nurse notes her urine output has fallen to 1.2 mL/kg/h and her morning bloods show Na 124 mmol/L (down from 137 yesterday), K 3.8, urea 3.2 mmol/L, creatinine 28 micromol/L, plasma osmolality 252 mOsm/kg. A paired urine sample shows urine sodium 78 mmol/L and urine osmolality 510 mOsm/kg. On clinical examination she is alert, euvolaemic with capillary refill of 2 seconds, moist mucous membranes, no peripheral oedema, JVP not raised, and no postural drop. She has had a mild morning headache but no vomiting, no seizure activity and no focal neurological signs.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92 bpm, BP 108/66 mmHg, T 37.0 deg C, GCS 14, weight 25 kg
data_table.Examination: Alert, euvolaemic clinically, no oedema, JVP not raised, no focal neurology, fontanelle closed
data_table.Investigations: Na 124 mmol/L, K 3.8, urea 3.2, plasma osm 252 mOsm/kg, urine Na 78 mmol/L, urine osm 510 mOsm/kg, urine output 1.2 mL/kg/h

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Fluid restriction to approximately two-thirds maintenance with daily electrolytes and strict fluid balance
options.rationale: Euvolaemic hyponatraemia with urine osmolality > plasma osmolality and urine sodium >40 mmol/L in a non-seizing asymptomatic child meets criteria for SIADH, treated initially with fluid restriction (typically two-thirds maintenance) and serial monitoring per RCH CPG: Hyponatraemia and Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

options.text: 3% hypertonic saline 3-5 mL/kg IV bolus to correct sodium by 5 mmol/L over 30 minutes
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline is reserved for symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizures, severely altered mental state) at sodium <125 mmol/L; she has a mild headache only and is alert per RCH CPG: Hyponatraemia.

options.text: Increase maintenance fluid to 1.5 times maintenance with 0.9% saline to replace renal sodium losses
options.rationale: Increasing fluid worsens dilutional hyponatraemia in SIADH; this is not cerebral salt wasting (which is hypovolaemic) per Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

options.text: Commence oral tolvaptan 15 mg daily for selective V2 receptor antagonism
options.rationale: Tolvaptan is rarely used in paediatrics, requires inpatient initiation, and is not first-line for transient post-meningitis SIADH per BMJ Best Practice: SIADH.

options.text: Administer furosemide 1 mg/kg IV with potassium chloride supplementation and continue current fluids
options.rationale: Furosemide is sometimes adjunctive in chronic SIADH refractory to restriction, but first-line is fluid restriction; routine furosemide is not indicated per Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

explanation.summary: SIADH is a common complication of CNS infection in children. Diagnostic criteria require euvolaemia, hypoosmolar plasma (<275), inappropriately concentrated urine (>100 mOsm/kg, usually >plasma), urine Na >40 mmol/L, normal renal/adrenal/thyroid function. First-line treatment is fluid restriction to two-thirds maintenance. Symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizure, severely altered mental state) is treated with 3% saline 3-5 mL/kg bolus aiming to raise sodium by 4-6 mmol/L. Correction rate should not exceed 8-10 mmol/L per 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination.
key_points: Fluid restriction is first-line if asymptomatic
3% saline 3-5 mL/kg only if symptomatic (seizures, GCS drop)
Differentiate from cerebral salt wasting (CSW is hypovolaemic with high urine output)
Correction rate max 8-10 mmol/L per 24 hours
explanation.pearls: Iso-osmolar hyponatraemia in a meningitis admission is more often hospital-acquired from hypotonic IV fluids than true SIADH; always check what's running.

stem: An 18-month-old boy of South Asian heritage is referred by his GP for evaluation of bowing of the legs noticed by his parents over the past 4 months. He has been delayed in walking, taking his first independent steps only at 17 months. His diet is largely breast milk and pureed vegetables with limited dairy and no oily fish. His mother wears full-coverage clothing for cultural reasons and has had no formal vitamin D supplementation during the pregnancy. The family lives in Melbourne. He has had no fractures, no fevers, and no chronic GI symptoms. On examination he has frontal bossing, palpable rachitic rosary at the costochondral junctions, widened wrist epiphyses, and clear bowing of both tibiae. He has mild hypotonia. Bloods today show calcium 2.05 mmol/L (corrected, reference 2.20-2.65), phosphate 0.9 mmol/L (reference 1.3-2.3), alkaline phosphatase 1450 U/L (reference <350), 25-OH vitamin D 18 nmol/L, parathyroid hormone 28 pmol/L (reference 1.5-7), creatinine 22 micromol/L. Wrist X-ray shows splaying, cupping and fraying of the distal radial metaphysis.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110 bpm, RR 28 /min, T 36.8 deg C, weight 25th centile, length 10th centile
data_table.Examination: Frontal bossing, rachitic rosary, widened wrists, tibial bowing, mild hypotonia, no fractures
data_table.Investigations: Ca 2.05 mmol/L, PO4 0.9 mmol/L, ALP 1450 U/L, 25-OH vit D 18 nmol/L, PTH 28 pmol/L, creatinine 22 micromol/L, wrist XR: cupping/fraying/splaying

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate treatment regimen?

options.text: Oral cholecalciferol 1000-2000 IU daily for 3 months plus oral elemental calcium 500 mg daily, then re-evaluate biochemistry
options.rationale: Nutritional vitamin D deficiency rickets in a child >12 months is treated with oral cholecalciferol 1000-2000 IU daily for at least 3 months (or a single high-dose stoss regimen of 150,000-300,000 IU) plus dietary calcium of approximately 500 mg/day, with biochemical and radiological follow-up per RCH CPG: Vitamin D Deficiency and APEG/ANZBMS position.
source_refs: APEG/ANZBMS Position Statement: Vitamin D in Children

options.text: Oral calcitriol 0.25 microg daily and oral phosphate Joulie's solution 30 mg/kg/day in divided doses
options.rationale: Calcitriol plus phosphate is the regimen for X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets, not nutritional rickets; nutritional rickets responds to cholecalciferol per APEG/ANZBMS position.
source_refs: APEG/ANZBMS Position Statement: Vitamin D in Children

options.text: Intramuscular ergocalciferol 600,000 IU single dose with no further supplementation and review in 12 months
options.rationale: Single high-dose IM regimens carry a risk of hypercalcaemia and are no longer preferred; oral daily or oral stoss dosing is safer and equally effective per RCH CPG: Vitamin D Deficiency.

options.text: Renal phosphate-wasting workup with FGF-23 and bicarbonate-citrate alkali therapy
options.rationale: The PTH is elevated (secondary hyperparathyroidism in nutritional deficiency); FGF-23-mediated phosphaturia is suggested when 25-OH vitamin D is normal and PTH is normal/low, not in this picture per APEG/ANZBMS.
source_refs: APEG/ANZBMS Position Statement: Vitamin D in Children

options.text: Restrict dietary phosphate and commence a low-calcium formula to reduce PTH drive
options.rationale: Low-calcium and low-phosphate diets worsen rickets; the deficient nutrient is vitamin D and calcium needs to be supplied not restricted per Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

explanation.summary: Nutritional vitamin D deficiency rickets shows hypocalcaemia, hypophosphataemia, raised ALP, raised PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism), and low 25-OH vitamin D (<30 nmol/L, with <50 nmol/L deficient per AU guidance). Treatment is cholecalciferol 1000-2000 IU/day for children 1-18 years for 3 months, plus dietary calcium. Maternal/household supplementation and exclusion of underlying malabsorption (coeliac, CF) should be considered.
key_points: AU 25-OH vit D cut-offs: deficient <30 nmol/L, insufficient 30-49, sufficient >=50
Treatment dose: <1 month 400 IU/day, 1-12 months 400-1000 IU/day, >1 year 1000-2000 IU/day for 3 months
Stoss therapy (single high-dose) reserved for adherence concerns
Check calcium 1-2 weeks into treatment for hungry bone hypocalcaemia
explanation.pearls: Screen the mother and siblings; antenatal maternal deficiency is the commonest source. Refer dietary, cultural, and sun-exposure causes together.

sources.label: APEG/ANZBMS Position Statement: Vitamin D in Children

stem: A 14-year-old boy is referred to a paediatric weight management clinic with a BMI of 36 kg/m^2 (>99.9th centile), with weight gain accelerating since age 9. He was a normally grown infant with a healthy weight until age 8. He has no syndromic features, normal growth velocity, normal pubertal progression, and no developmental delay. He has obstructive sleep apnoea (AHI 18 events/h on polysomnography 6 months ago), insulin-resistant acanthosis nigricans at the neck, persistent right knee pain on impact, and self-reported low mood with school avoidance for the last 6 months. His mother and uncle have type 2 diabetes; his father has had bariatric surgery. Investigations show fasting glucose 5.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.9%, fasting insulin elevated, ALT 78 U/L with hepatic steatosis on ultrasound, normal TSH and free T4, normal cortisol with no Cushingoid features, normal calcium and phosphate, and normal renal function. He attends a structured family-based lifestyle programme weekly. His parents ask whether medication should be added.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 80 bpm, BP 132/82 mmHg, T 36.7 deg C, BMI 36 kg/m^2 (>99.9th centile)
data_table.Examination: Acanthosis nigricans neck and axillae, no striae, normal pubertal stage (Tanner G4 P4), no goitre, no dysmorphic features
data_table.Investigations: Fasting glucose 5.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.9%, fasting insulin raised, ALT 78 U/L, US: hepatic steatosis, TSH/free T4 normal, cortisol normal, AHI 18/h

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in pharmacological management?

options.text: Commence oral metformin 500 mg twice daily titrated to 1000 mg twice daily as an adjunct to ongoing intensive lifestyle therapy
options.rationale: Metformin is the only oral agent approved for use in paediatric obesity-related insulin resistance with prediabetes/NAFLD as an adjunct to intensive lifestyle change in adolescents from age 10, particularly with HbA1c in the prediabetic range, hepatic steatosis and acanthosis nigricans per APEG position on adolescent metformin use and NHMRC obesity guidance.
source_refs: APEG Position Statement: Metformin in Adolescents
NHMRC Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Management of Overweight and Obesity

options.text: Commence orlistat 120 mg three times daily with meals as the preferred pharmacotherapy for adolescent obesity
options.rationale: Orlistat has limited efficacy and significant GI side effects in adolescents, and is not first-line in Australian paediatric practice per NHMRC obesity guidance.
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Management of Overweight and Obesity

options.text: Refer immediately for sleeve gastrectomy without further trial of medical therapy
options.rationale: Bariatric surgery in Australian paediatric practice is considered after failure of intensive medical and lifestyle therapy at BMI >40 (or >35 with comorbidities), with multidisciplinary assessment; it is not the immediate next step per ANZMOSS adolescent position.
source_refs: ANZMOSS Position Statement on Adolescent Bariatric Surgery

options.text: Commence liraglutide 3 mg subcutaneously daily as TGA-approved first-line adolescent obesity therapy
options.rationale: GLP-1 agonists are emerging in adolescent obesity (US FDA approval for liraglutide and semaglutide age >=12) but in Australia TGA paediatric approval and PBS subsidy are limited, and metformin remains first-line where prediabetes/NAFLD is present per APEG position.
source_refs: APEG Position Statement: Metformin in Adolescents

options.text: Add a low-dose thyroid hormone supplement to increase basal metabolic rate
options.rationale: Thyroid hormone supplementation in a euthyroid child is contraindicated and causes iatrogenic hyperthyroidism per Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

explanation.summary: Australian management of adolescent obesity is structured family-based intensive lifestyle intervention (diet, activity, behaviour, sleep, screen time) with treatment of comorbidities (OSA, NAFLD, mental health). Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive: metformin is the most evidence-based oral agent for insulin resistance, prediabetes and NAFLD. Bariatric surgery (sleeve gastrectomy) is considered in selected adolescents with BMI >40 (or >35 with comorbidities) after multidisciplinary review. Always exclude secondary causes (hypothyroidism, Cushing, Prader-Willi, MC4R) before labelling primary obesity.
key_points: Metformin 500 mg BD, titrate to max 1000 mg BD if tolerated
Address OSA, NAFLD, mental health and joint pain in parallel
Bariatric criteria (AU): BMI >40 or >35 with comorbidity, age >=15-16, failed intensive therapy
Edmonton Obesity Staging System for Paediatrics (EOSS-P) for risk stratification
explanation.pearls: Acanthosis nigricans + prediabetic HbA1c in an adolescent is a strong indication for metformin; do not wait for overt T2DM to act.

sources.label: APEG Position Statement: Metformin in Adolescents
NHMRC Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Management of Overweight and Obesity
ANZMOSS Position Statement on Adolescent Bariatric Surgery
